Legacy. A sense that after they have done their work, be it a project, a job, or a career, the world is different in a way that is meaningful to them. Mastery. A deepening of skills, strengths, and talents that they feel help define them and their identity. This includes the increasing responsibility that comes with expertise and experience. Freedom. They want to get paid what they are worth, but they value things like remote work, flexible hours, and great benefits more than the actual size of their paycheck.
he was tired of only designing in blue and white. As a designer, he craved a bigger palette; he wanted to be able to use red, green, and purple.
They only see what is right in front of them and fail to understand the importance of building a market rather than just a product or service.

title: The Rabbit Factor: The tense, hilarious bestseller from the ‘Funniest writer in Europe’ … FIRST in a series and soon to be a major motion picture
author: Antti Tuomainen and David Hackston

verses.text: Though banks have a habit of continuously and systematically making their customer service worse, they haven’t quite reached the point where they send knife-throwers after their debtors in the dead of night.

title: The Reading List: Emotional and uplifting, escape in 2023 with the most heartwarming debut fiction novel
author: Sara Nisha Adams

verses.text: Sometimes, books just take us away for a little while, and return us to our place with a new perspective.
‘How happy and good we’d be if we had no worries …’
There was something magical in that – in sharing a world you have loved; allowing someone to see it through the same pair of spectacles you saw it through yourself.
Looking back now, he was pleased they had gone to the beach, on a whim. But he wished he’d said he loved her every single day.
Sometimes the sky is grey Sometimes the day’s grey too But behind every grey sky There’s always some blue

title: The Ride of a Lifetime: Lessons in Creative Leadership from 15 Years as CEO of the Walt Disney Company
author: Robert Iger

verses.text: Integrity. Nothing is more important than the quality and integrity of an organization’s people and its product. A company’s success depends on setting high ethical standards for all things, big and small. Another way of saying this is: The way you do anything is the way you do everything.

In your work, in your life, you’ll be more respected and trusted by the people around you if you honestly own up to your mistakes. It’s impossible not to make them; but it is possible to acknowledge them, learn from them, and set an example that it’s okay to get things wrong sometimes.
You have to ask the questions you need to ask, admit without apology what you don’t understand, and do the work to learn what you need to learn as quickly as you can.
As a leader, you should want those around you to be eager to rise up and take on more responsibility, as long as dreaming about the job they want doesn’t distract them from the job they have.

Optimism sets a different machine in motion. Especially in difficult moments, the people you lead need to feel confident in your ability to focus on what matters, and not to operate from a place of defensiveness and self-preservation. This isn’t about saying things are good when they’re not, and it’s not about conveying some innate faith that “things will work out.” It’s about believing you and the people around you can steer toward the best outcome, and not communicating the feeling that all is lost if things don’t break your way. The tone you set as a leader has an enormous effect on the people around you. No one wants to follow a pessimist.
Don’t let your ego get in the way of making the best possible decision.
If you approach and engage people with respect and empathy, the seemingly impossible can become real.

PEOPLE SOMETIMES SHY AWAY from taking big swings because they assess the odds and build a case against trying something before they even take the first step. One of the things I’ve always instinctively felt—and something that was greatly reinforced working for people like Roone and Michael—is that long shots aren’t usually as long as they seem.

title: The School of Greatness: A Real-World Guide to Living Bigger, Loving Deeper, and Leaving a Legacy
author: Lewis Howes

verses.text: One of my teachers, the author and journalist Steven Kotler, would later define greatness as “waking up every day and saying ‘Okay. Today I’m going to move mountains.’”
“Greatness means having pride in yourself, being happy with yourself, knowing you’ve worked for something and couldn’t have done anything more. That is greatness itself.”
If you don’t give yourself a moment to visualize the clear results you want to create, then you are less likely to achieve what you desire.
“You need desire to be fully alive and you need vision to fulfill your desires.”
If you want to be great at anything, you’ve got to have a clear vision of exactly what you want, why you want it, and when you want it to happen.
Your job is to create a vision that makes you want to jump out of bed in the morning. If it doesn’t, go back to bed until you have a bigger dream.
The seasons where coaches had us write out our team vision and our personal goals were the most successful seasons I ever had.
Download your Certificate of Achievement at schoolofgreatness.com/resources and you will receive an easy template for completing this exercise in excellence.
In this exercise, your job is to map out what your perfect day looks like along the path to achieving your vision. There are two parts to this exercise: the macro and the micro.
You are declaring to yourself and to your world that this list of five principles is what you stand for and live by, no matter what comes your way.

On a blank piece of paper, write the following (to download a sample worksheet, go to schoolofgreatness.com/resources): Your name. Today’s date. 6 or 12 months from today. Answer these questions. Who am I? What do I stand for? What is my vision for myself, my family, and the world? List your five principles (Personal Principles Declaration)
You can go as slow as you need to go, but you cannot stop. You can never give up or drop out of giving your best in your life.
Allow yourself to relax and be calm. Breathe relaxing breaths. Now visualize whatever you want to see as complete. Nothing negative, only positive outcomes.

15-Second Centering Breath Process 1.Breathe in through the nose if you can for a count of one, two, three, four, and five, expanding the belly. 2.Then hold it for a count of one and two. 3.Then breathe out through the mouth for a count of one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, and eight, releasing the air in the belly.
If you want an another free guided meditation from me to get you in the right frame of mind for your day, then check out schoolofgreatness.com/resources for additional resources.
Each night in your gratitude journal, be mindful of what’s really working for you and what isn’t working.
“Do not despise the bottom rungs in the ascent to greatness,”
“If you want to be the best, you have to do things that other people aren’t willing to do.”

Think about your vision and your goals. Imagine the hustle required to make them a reality. Now write down all of the things you are afraid of if you throw yourself headlong into the hustle. Allow yourself to experience the feeling of fear while you write these things down.
Now redirect each what-if into a potential positive outcome.
Do one thing every day that makes your body healthier and stronger.
Do something every day to improve your mindset and your way of thinking.
Write a list of 10 skills you want to learn. Start with the one that excites you the most and create a game plan for how you will learn it over the next 6 months.

The famous English billionaire businessman and adventurer Richard Branson has been asked for his best piece of business advice. His answer is always one word: exercise. Why? Because if you don’t take care of yourself, you can’t take care of your business.
Our capacity for success and greatness is embodied by the people we surround ourselves with.
Create three columns with the following headlines: •Things you don’t like doing •Things you can’t do •Things you shouldn’t do
“Greatness to me is just about being there for other people; living a life that is others oriented is where you achieve greatness.”
Write down something simple you can do today to give back to your community, family, and friends or a stranger.
Put your own mask on first. When you make sure your needs are met and you are full, then you’ll have even more energy to give to others. Go out and live a life of service!

title: The Science of Storytelling: Why Stories Make Us Human, and How to Tell Them Better
author: Will Storr

verses.text: This is what storytellers do. They create moments of unexpected change that seize the attention of their protagonists and, by extension, their readers and viewers.
The place of maximum curiosity – the zone in which storytellers play – is when people think they have some idea but aren’t quite sure.

In his paper ‘The Psychology of Curiosity’, Loewenstein breaks down four ways of involuntarily inducing curiosity in humans: (1) the ‘posing of a question or presentation of a puzzle’; (2) ‘exposure to a sequence of events with an anticipated but unknown resolution’; (3) ‘the violation of expectations that triggers a search for an explanation’; (4) knowledge of ‘possession of information by someone else’.
Active grammar means readers model the scene on the page in the same way that they’d model it if it happened in front of them.

One study concluded that, to make vivid scenes, three specific qualities of an object should be described, with the researcher’s examples including ‘a dark blue carpet’ and ‘an orange striped pencil.’

‘Any scene which does not both advance the plot and standalone (that is, dramatically, by itself, on its own merits) is either superfluous or incorrectly written,’ he wrote. ‘Start, every time, with this inviolable rule: the scene must be dramatic. It must start because the hero has a problem, and it must culminate with the hero finding him or herself either thwarted or educated that another way exists.’
‘You want all your scenes to have a “because” between them, and not an “and then”.’

When designing a character, it’s often useful to think of them in terms of their theory of control. How have they learned to control the world? When unexpected change strikes, what’s their automatic go-to tactic for wrestling with the chaos? What’s their default, flawed response? The answer, as we’ve just seen, comes from that character’s core beliefs about reality, the precious and fiercely defended ideas around which they’ve formed their sense of self.
An ignition point is the first event in a cause-and-effect sequence that will ultimately force the protagonist to question their deepest beliefs.

Researchers have found that violence and cruelty has four general causes: greed and ambition; sadism; high self-esteem and moral idealism. Popular belief and clichéd stories tend to have it that greed and sadism are dominant. In fact, they’re vanishingly small. It’s actually high self-esteem and moral idealism – convictions of personal and moral superiority – that drive most acts of evil.

Who is this person? This is the question all stories ask. It emerges first at the ignition point. When the initial change strikes, the protagonist overreacts or behaves in an otherwise unexpected way. We sit up, suddenly attentive. Who is this person who behaves like this? The question then re-emerges every time the protagonist is challenged by the plot and compelled to make a choice.

title: The Secrets of Flowers: A heart-warming novel for 2026 from the Sunday Times bestselling author of The Keeper of Stories and The Book of Beginnings
author: Sally Page

verses.text: think flowers matter in some way. We fill houses – and ships with them; we use them to send messages; we grow them; we even eat them. Once you see that, you start to see flowers everywhere.’
Flowers say, “I will always love you, my friend”.’
She knows you cannot choose the time you die. Death takes the young and the old. But still there is her own guilt. The guilt of simply living.

title: The Six Conversations of a Brilliant Manager
author: Alan J. Sears

verses.text: Conversation #1: ‘What can you do about that?’)
Coaching – and there it all was. What the other person wants to do, where they are now, how they could do it and what they are prepared to do about it.
Conversation #2: ‘Who should really own this?’

‘That doesn’t mean I am going to leave you in the lurch,’ Sam pointed out. ‘It means that I will work with you to help you find ways to solve any problems you have, and you will then go off and take the necessary actions. We can agree what you are going to do, and how far you can go, every time, so you can be sure what you’ve got my authority for and what you haven’t.’
Conversation #3: ‘How should we be behaving?’)
Conversation #4: ‘Who’s really doing this?’)
Conversation #5: ‘Where are we heading?’)
Conversation #6: ‘How are we doing?’)

I had been treating it as something I was supposed to produce because that was my job. It hadn’t occurred to me to make it something I could be really proud of, something that really added to the business, something where I could say: “Hey, that was mine – I did that!”

So the first thing to do is try to frame the conversation more positively to yourself. Instead of ‘giving some tough feedback’ you could think of ‘having a highly developmental conversation’. Instead of telling someone this is their last chance, you could think of helping them save their job.

1.State the problem behaviour. 2.Say why it is a problem. 3.Say what you expect. 4.Ask for their perspective. 5.Ask for their solution(s). 6.If you can accept their solution, do. If not, say what solution you can accept. 7.Summarise the solution and agree when you will review it.
The most important thing to remember is that anything you delegate will not be done exactly the way you would have done it.

title: The Start-up of You: Adapt, Take Risks, Grow Your Network, and Transform Your Life
author: Reid Hoffman and Ben Casnocha

verses.text: All humans are entrepreneurs not because they should start companies but because the will to create and take control of our destiny is encoded in human DNA—and creation is the essence of entrepreneurship.

Finished ought to be an F-word for all of us. We are all works in progress. Each day presents an opportunity to learn more, do more, be more, grow more in our lives and careers. We believe it is essential to keep your career in “permanent beta.”

Some have pointed out the mindset shift that can be brought about simply by adding the word yet to the end of a sentence that describes something you haven’t done in the world. “I haven’t taken a big risk in my career—yet.” “I don’t know much about life sciences and biology—yet.” Permanent beta is about the power of yet.

If you want to chart a course that differentiates you from other professionals in the marketplace, the first step is being able to complete the sentence “A company would hire me over other professionals because …” How are you faster, better, or cheaper than other people who want to do what you’re doing in the world? What are you offering that’s hard to come by?
What combination of capabilities do you possess that are both rare and valuable, and thus impossible for the market to ignore?4 That’s your ticket.
for most of us it’s easier to be in the top 10 percent skill area in two or more skills and blend them together than it is to shoot for world-class status in just one.

If you think you lack certain skills that would make you more competitive, don’t use that lack as an excuse for why you’re not getting ahead. Start developing them right now. Is It Time to Earn or Time to Learn? At this moment, is your primary goal to earn (money) or to learn (skills/knowledge/experiences)?
The reason specializing gives you an advantage is because there will be less competition—which magnifies the market value of your skill.

Low to Medium: It can be unexpectedly transformative to go from knowing nothing to knowing a little. That’s because creative aha moments occur when you make connections between disparate knowledge or skills. You need to know enough about a topic area—often it’s just an elementary level of awareness—for such a topic to be associative in your brain. You become a stronger strategic thinker and brainstormer when you know at least a little about a lot of topics.
Medium to High: Once you’re basically literate in a topic or skill area, investing more to become “good” rarely transforms your career.
High to World Class: Being “world class” is where legendary careers are made. But working to become even better at things you’re already good at is a counterintuitive idea for many people.
Invest in yourself at the low end and the high end of your existing skill set and knowledge base.

If you’re counting on a career skill that might be ahead of its time (“I’m going to become the leading expert in how VR/AR technology can transform the theme park industry”), just know that you’re running a higher-risk, higher-reward strategy.

As you think about which industries to work in, which skills to obtain, and which books to read, study the types of tech startups that were recently funded by venture capitalists at the Series A and Series B stages because these tend to be on the cutting edge of emerging trends. For example, Coinbase raised its Series A and Series B funding in 2013. If you invested in learning about cryptocurrencies in the years following Coin-base’s Series A, there would have been an abundance of life-changing crypto career opportunities for you, in part because at that time crypto knowledge was rare in a growing market. Study what venture capitalists are investing in to glimpse the trends or markets on the rise.

I very frequently get the question: “What’s going to change in the next 10 years?” And that is a very interesting question; it’s a very common one. I almost never get the question: “What’s not going to change in the next 10 years?” And I submit to you that that second question is actually the more important of the two—because you can build a business strategy around the things that are stable in time
Markets that don’t exist don’t care how smart you are.

In the startup world, competition—or lack thereof—makes a big difference. LinkedIn, from the outset, struck a different path from its social media competitors. The world of job searches and résumés is “boring” compared to other kinds of social networks where you can post pictures of your friends and invite them to parties. But less sexy markets often mean fewer talented competitors.

“Get out of the weeds. Go where there’s fast growth, because fast growth creates all opportunities.”2 It was outstanding advice: Work in a market with natural momentum, as we discussed in the preceding chapter. Ride the big waves. Some industries are growth loops in and of themselves, and that’s where you want to be.

No one’s ever truly ready to have kids, start a company, move to a new city, or pounce on a game-changing career opportunity. If you want any of those things, you have to make your move sooner—when you’re almost ready. Ronald Reagan is said to have argued that you don’t need someone to agree with you 100 percent for them to be “with you”—you just need them to be with you on 80 percent of the issues. If this rule were applied to your career it would mean you don’t need to be 100 percent sure of a decision—just 80 percent sure. Or 80 percent ready.

The best Plan B is different but very much related to what you’re already doing. As you think about your own Plan B alternatives, favor options that let you keep one foot planted while the other one swings to the new territory, ideally a sector that is teeming with opportunity. Pivot into an adjacent niche that is hotter than your current one.

The way to feel comfortable taking those big risks that lead to potentially life-changing opportunities is to have one fallback plan that is highly certain. That’s Plan Z: a reliable backup plan you shift to when you no longer have confidence in Plan A and Plan B, or when your plans get severely disrupted. The certainty of the Plan Z backstop is what enables you to be aggressive—not tentative—about Plans A and B. It’s psychologically empowering: With a Plan Z, you’ll at least know you can tolerate failure. Without it, you could be frozen in fear contemplating the worst-case scenarios.

Whatever your Plan Z is, think of it as a lifeboat, not a long-term plan. Invoking Plan Z should allow you to retreat, regroup, and develop an entirely new Plan A. If you’re resourceful enough, you might even find a way to turn this safety net into a catapult, which is the best-case scenario for a Plan Z.

Invest in Yourself In the next day: Make a list of the key uncertainties, doubts, and questions you have about your career at the present moment. It doesn’t need to be perfect and you don’t need to share the list with anyone else: just write down all your worries, raw and unfiltered, like you would in a journal. Get them out of your head and onto a document. Write out your Plan A (your current job or portfolio) and Plan Z (your worst-case fallback plan); then jot some notes about possible Plan B moves from your current situation. By writing them down, you’ll clarify your thinking. In the next week: Schedule a coffee meeting with someone who used to work in your professional niche who pivoted to a new career plan. Deconstruct what they did. How did he or she make the shift? Was it a good move? What were the signs that the time was right? Make a plan to develop one additional transferable skill—the type of skill that’s broadly useful to other potential jobs. Written and oral communication skills, general management experience, technical and computer skills, people smarts, and international experience or language skills are examples of skill assets with high option value—that is, they are transferable to a wide range of possible Plan Bs. Give yourself a year to get better at this one skill. In the next month: Time permitting, take on an experimental side project that you pursue during some nights and weekends. Orient it toward a skill or experience that is different but related—something that either enhances what you do now or can serve as a possible Plan B if your Plan A doesn’t work out. It could be as straightforward as a blog or email newsletter you publish weekly, or a side consulting gig for a friend. Ideally, collaborate on this project with someone else in your network. Diversify your portfolio just a bit. Establish an online identity independent of your employer. Reserve a personal domain name (yourname.com) and set up a branded email address that you use with your network separate from your current work email address. Reach out to five people who work in niches adjacent to yours (that is, the same industry but a different company or a slightly different industry but a similar type of company). Ask them to coffee (in person or on Zoom). Compare your Plan B with theirs. Keep up these relationships over time so you can benefit from diverse perspectives, and so you’re in a better position to potentially pivot to those niches when necessary.
Relationships matter to your career no matter the organization or level of seniority you find yourself in, because every job boils down to people.
Here’s one of the most important self-improvement principles of all: The fastest way to change yourself is to hang out with people who are already the way you want to be.

“I” versus “We” is a false choice. Both the individual and the team matter. Your career success depends on both your personal capabilities and your network’s ability to magnify them. These are inseparable. Think of it as IWe. An individual’s power is raised exponentially with the help of a team (a network). But just as zero to the one hundredth power is still zero, there’s no team without the individual.
To sum up, as you meet your friends and new people, shift from asking yourself the very natural question of “What’s in it for me?” and ask instead, “What’s in it for us?”

There’s a saying that business moves at the speed of trust. With allies, you can rely upon the transitive property of trust—an ally’s references can help expand your circle of collaborators more quickly, enabling you to tackle ambitious new projects like writing a book.

Strong ties usually introduce redundancy in knowledge, activities, and people. In contrast, weak ties usually sit outside of your inner circle and have access to different people and knowledge. Thus, there’s a greater likelihood a weak tie will be exposed to information or opportunities that you aren’t. It’s not that weak ties per se find you jobs; weak ties in and of themselves are not especially valuable. What is valuable is the breadth and reach of your network—the information corridors that open via the people you know.

When you reach out to someone to request an introduction, be clear about how you intend to help the person to whom you’re being introduced—or at least how you’ll ensure it’s not a waste of their time. This will reassure the person making the introduction that they’ll actually accrue social capital (or at least not burn it) by granting your request. Figuring out how you can help the person you want to connect with—or at least figuring out the tightest point of mutual interest—does take some legwork, but it pays off.

How to do a cold email well? Author Ramit Sethi highlights three attributes of emails that cause busy people to actually reply and help: There are very specific, personalized, and insightful questions that cannot be found via a quick Google search. These questions take up 90 percent of the email, instead of the email being a rambling sales pitch about the person’s background, talents, and career uncertainties. They never outright ask for a job or money—they know that “success” with the initial cold email is generating a second conversation or meeting, not completing a transaction all at once. And we’ll add our own, fourth recommendation: Risk being funny. In the daily slog that is email life, a little humor stands out. And research shows us it makes you come off as smart. Cold approaches are a delicate art with low probability of success (relative to warm introductions) but if they work they can be life-changing. Be bold. Try it. Occasionally the power of IWe can explode when two strangers take a chance on each other.
If the best way to strengthen a relationship is to help the other person, the second-best way is to let yourself be helped.
So, upon moving to Seattle, he declared that $7,000 of those savings would be “California money”—or what we called an “Interesting People Fund.”

Edgy is interesting, and interesting wins eyeballs. Most of us have provocative ideas or beliefs inside us; the issue is that we get scared to share them. Don’t be afraid. An observation that makes you a bit uncomfortable, one that causes you to hesitate before clicking “publish,” is precisely the sort of observation that will win people’s attention in a noisy social media feed. If it’s your ego that’s stopping you—Do people really care about what I of all people have to say on this topic?—remember that most content on the internet is actually quite boring. What you have to say is almost certainly more interesting than the vast majority of posts in your followers’ feeds.

In the same way that reciprocity is key to your relationships with your allies and friendlies, if you want to gain followers and spark cycles of engagement online, you have to follow and engage with others. Praise things that other people do. “Like” their content. Repost it. Participate in the goodwill economy on social media and genuinely try to help others advance.

Imagine you got laid off from your job today. Who are the ten people you’d email for advice on what to do next? Reach out to them now, when you don’t need anything specifically. “Dig the well before you’re thirsty.”
Post something on social media that pushes the envelope, even something that makes you a bit uncomfortable. Find five new interesting people to follow.

Create an “interesting people fund” to which you automatically funnel a certain percentage of your paycheck. Use it to pay for coffees, lunches, and the occasional plane ticket to meet new people and shore up existing relationships.
Serendipity involves being alert to potential opportunity and acting on it. You have to cultivate it.

Courting randomness can be as simple as seeking out more information than your peers do. It’s simple math: The more information you consume, the more likely you are to happen upon something that will light your mind on fire.

As my dad once told me, making a decision reduces opportunities in the short run, but increases opportunities in the long run. To move forward in your career, you have to commit to specific opportunities even in the face of doubt and inconvenience.

A couple decades ago, a professor at NYU named Zur Shapira asked some seven hundred high-level executives to describe how they think about risk in different scenarios. What he found did not come as a surprise to us; nor would it be a surprise to any business executive with real-world experience. The executives he surveyed didn’t crunch the numbers and calculate the expected mathematical probabilities of various scenarios. They didn’t draft long lists of pros and cons, nor did they draw intricate decision trees. Instead, most of them simply tried to get a handle on a single yes-or-no question: Could they tolerate the outcome if the worst-case scenario happened? That’s it. Yes or no. It’s the first thing you want to ask of a possible opportunity: If the worst-case scenario comes to pass, would I still be in the game? If I were pummeled, could I get back up? If the doomsday scenario is the serious and indelible tarnishing of your reputation, or the loss of all your economic assets, or something that is truly career ending (rather than something that may seem career ending but in reality would simply require you to pivot), don’t accept that risk. If the worst-case scenario is getting fired, losing a little bit of time or money, or experiencing some uncomfortable emotions, as long as you have a solid and reliable Plan Z in place, you can recover resiliently. Consider other factors, but remain open to taking on that risk.

Jeff Bezos calls this overall idea the difference between “one-way doors” and “two-way doors.” One-way doors are those you walk through and then can never look back. Two-way doors allow you to reverse course if needed. One-way doors, in other words, carry a higher degree of risk. Neither door is inherently better or worse than the other, but you should think hard about the risk before stepping over that threshold.

If something worthwhile will be riskier in five years than it is now, be more aggressive and seize the day. As you age and build more assets, your risk tolerance usually shifts toward increased caution—that is, unless you decide you want a career transition or lifestyle upgrade, as more and more people do.

Jobs that pay less but offer tremendous learning. People tend to focus on easily quantifiable hard assets, like how much they’re getting paid in cash when weighing career opportunities. Jobs that offer less cash but more learning or great networking yield a high reward yet are sometimes too quickly dismissed as risky.

Hiring someone without much experience but who’s a fast learner. This is a medium risk with high potential reward: Fast learners can make up for their inexperience. Moreover, they tend to be undervalued by the market and are likely to be cheaper to hire.
What you get when you connect to other people’s brains is called network intelligence.

Today, however, even search literacy is not enough. It’s still critically important, yes, but insufficient. The bigger advantage is gained by network literacy: knowing how to conceptualize, access, and benefit from the information flowing through your web of relationships, both online and off.

One way to start is by identifying who in your network might be able to help with career decisions. Then sort those people into three (at times overlapping) categories: Domain experts. These are the pros, the people who really know the ins and outs of a specific, relevant topic. Got a question about negotiating your salary? Ask your friend who’s hired dozens of people. “You” experts. These are the people who know you well. Your mother. Your childhood friend. They may not be up on the latest industry happenings, but they have a good sense of your priorities, personality, and personal history. They can help you unpack feelings of confusion and sometimes even intuit how you’ll likely feel about various outcomes of your decision. Free-range experts. These are just really smart people you trust. They may not be domain experts in the specific topic area and may not know you well. But occasionally their sheer analytical horsepower and/or wide-ranging wisdom can be useful. At the least, whatever a really smart outsider says stands a chance of being completely different from anything else you’ll hear.

As a general rule, when you want information from your network to help you make a career decision, begin by asking domain experts. Next, talk to people with whom you have strong personal relationships; notice how Iris Wong talked to her father only after talking to a friend with years of experience in a business similar to hers. Then, time permitting, turn to really smart outsiders.

It’s useful to have a stash of good questions always at the ready. The economist Tyler Cowen likes to ask Ben, every time he sees him, “What’s the most interesting thing you’ve learned over the past few months?” Other questions that are all-purpose and useful include: What’s keeping you up at night? What’s a good book you’ve read recently? What bad habit are you trying to overcome? What’s a trend in your industry that wouldn’t be obvious to outsiders? What have you changed your mind about recently? Are you following any new and interesting voices on Twitter? What’s something hotly debated in your field?
Decisions about whom to work with are the most important decisions in your career.

If you’re evaluating a new career opportunity, it’s vital to reference-check your prospective manager before saying yes. If you’re applying for a fairly senior position, you can ask your would-be boss directly for references.

In her excellent book Bird by Bird, Anne Lamott cites another writer, E. L. Doctorow, who said: “Writing a novel is like driving a car at night. You can see only as far as your headlights, but you can make the whole trip that way.” Then Lamott layers on her own wisdom: “You don’t have to see where you’re going, you don’t have to see your destination or everything you will pass along the way. You just have to see two or three feet ahead of you.”

On the book’s website, startupofyou.com, you’ll find more information and advanced strategies for how to invest in yourself, strengthen your network, and transform your career. You’ll also be able to connect with other professionals, also in permanent beta, who will help you move from ideas to action, from knowledge to implementation. On Twitter, you can find us at @startupofyou or at @reidhoffman and @bencasnocha. Tag us in your tweets about ABZ Planning, networks, competitive advantage, or any other idea from the book. We’ll reply to and promote the best questions, comments, or ideas that circulate on LinkedIn and Twitter.

title: The Storyteller's Spellbook: How to make your ideas more compelling and your career more magical
author: James Whittaker

verses.text: Your purpose is not to inform your audience. Your purpose is to take them on a journey.
enjoy the videos and accompanying material at www.docjamesw.com/storytelling.
The characters invite them to ask what if I knew someone that this happened to? That is precisely the wizardry of Character Vision.
The idea behind casting a Neural Blast is to grab the attention of the audiences’ brain function by changing something about your oratory.

A sudden pitch change in your voice makes them listen. Their brains are like hey something just happened here, better listen up! A dramatic gesture followed by a moment of silence will also get their attention. Speaking faster and then slower puts them on notice. Raising your voice in a passionate plea creates that same passion in their own mind. Lowering your voice conspiratorially will make them listen more closely. Words that conjure images in their heads also put them on notice.
Did they not understand that they don’t get to decide how special they are? That only the audience gets to make that determination?
I call it Charm Audience and it should be cast first in nearly any presentation you give. It’s based on a critical piece of storyteller lore: that the concept wins an audience, not the details.
You can only cast Find Familiar if you do the research about your audience and know their story, background and culture.
Capturing an audience rarely happens by accident. You need to put more time into the Charm Audience Spell than any other in your entire talk.

Seriously, if there is only one thing you remember from this book (or from anything you ever read about storytelling) it is this: that your job as a storyteller is to take your audience on a journey to a place of more knowledge and better understanding than when they began.
If there is one place in your presentation that you can’t afford to get wrong, it is the beginning.
Simply thinking hard about this offer is going to put you in the top 10% of speakers

The next step is to motivate them to act. After all, what good is it to sit through a presentation only to leave it and go back to the same life you had before? If your purpose isn’t to get them to act, why are you there? Why are they there?
Profundity to open their minds and Repetition to help develop an idea in their minds.
Quoting Arthur C. Clarke, or any other recognizable or famous name, is the Summon Authority Spell, a powerful way to convey a message.

Suspense activates the mind. It begs questions to be asked and answers to be guessed. It opens neural pathways as the listeners’ brains anticipate the outcome. Most good stories have some element of suspense, otherwise they become predictable. Predictable is dull.
Now, the most powerful spell in this story is Expose Moral. The idea is to produce a teachable moment that will help the audience grow even as you tell them a story.
Expose Moral can be powerful enough to change the course of the audiences’ lives and careers. Use with extreme caution.
You have to create time for your own priorities. That way, some of your effort accrues to your own career as opposed to enriching your manager and company.

The new spell I want to introduce you to is the Behold Spell which delivers advice not as a lecture but as a demonstration. In others words, show don’t tell. This spell requires that you think about the technique, lesson, method … whatever concept you want to get across and instead of telling the audience about it, show them an example.

The questions your audiences ask need good answers. If you manage to become good onstage and yet can’t field questions smoothly and authoritatively, then the spells you wove as a speaker will dissipate during a subpar Q&A.
The Get Personal Spell means revealing something about your life, your loves, your habits or your psyche to your audience. The more personal, the more powerful.
The best way to do this is to share negative or neutral emotions rather than positive ones.
Metaphors are methods of describing an unfamiliar concept (like privacy in my story) by relating it to a familiar concept (like the NSA and Santa Claus) that the audience understands.
A metaphor only works when you use something instantly familiar that the audience doesn’t have to think about.
DESCRIBING WHAT YOU DO for a living is an important story waiting to be told. Indeed, it may be the most requested story you will ever tell.
Wanna be remembered? Know your story and have it at the ready. Someone important will ask.
See me actually give this talk at https://docs.com/james-whittaker/7717/the-art-of-stagepresence.

For the duration of your presentation there is only one time when you have the entire audience’s undivided attention without having to earn it. It’s those crucial first 30-60 seconds where everyone is fresh, everyone is attentive and waiting to hear from you. Do. Not. Waste. It.
DON’T introduce yourself.
Simply ask yourself what is the most interesting thing you have to say? Look through your entire presentation and find the most interesting, intriguing, inspiring part you can and say it first.
DO inform them of what they are go ing to get out of your presentation.
DON’T tell a joke or start with a personal story.

Give your talk the “tweet test.” Describe your talk in 140 characters or less. This process will help you narrow and re fine your central message. The inability to do this means that have too much to remember.
The secret to this stage is quite simple: don’t be coy. State the value plain and simple up front.
Nothing helps an audience engage more deeply with you and your subject matter than a direct approach: tell them why they want what you have to offer and describe what it will do for them.
Content-wise, the trick is to occasionally remind them why they are here listening to you. Don’t let them lose sight of the central theme.
The faster you talk the more excitement you convey. The slower you talk the more serious your words seem.

A pause, correctly placed, coaxes the listeners to pay extra attention. Something is going to happen, they think, and thus the pause serves its purpose. But I find both sides of the pause to be useful in really waking up people’s brains. Make a statement. Then pause. Then clarify the statement you just made. The pause gives people a chance to think about the statement you made. They get to guess about how you might clarify it. Think about it. This is the exact format of every joke you have ever told or heard.
If a story has no purpose, it is simply entertainment. But the best stories, and very likely all the stories you will tell as part of your career, are a direct attempt to get people to act.
What are the things that should be most impactful? These are the things that you want to remind them of right at the end of the talk.
Finally, give them homework!
Every time my nerves would surface, I wielded my opening line as a weapon against my flight response.

You are going to want to dote over them and fuss about the details all that much more. This means spells like Profundity, Repetition and Summon Authority are really important because they focus exclusively on the words you say.

Take the notes you develop in the ‘what you say’ section and color code them with intonation needs. Italicize the words you intend to emphasize using a different voice cadence. Bold the words you intend to raise your voice on. Use … a … notation … that … reminds … you … to … speak … slower.
Move closer to the camera when you are making an important point.
Have a story that answers the question “what do you do?”
